author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
The Fidelity of Memory; The Unsearchable Treasure of Memory; The Powers of a Man’s Understanding Sufficiently Understood by None.
title: The Fidelity of Memory; The Unsearchable Treasure of Memory; The Powers of a Man’s Understanding Sufficiently Understood by None.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: We often assume that we shall retain a thing in our memory; and so thinking, we do not write it down.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But afterwards, when we wish to recall it, it refuses to come to mind; and we are then sorry that we thought it would return to memory, or that we did not secure it in writing so as to prevent its escape; and lo, on a sudden, without our seeking it, it occurs to us.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Then does it follow that we were not ourselves when we thought this?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: And that we cease to be the same thing that we were, when we are no longer able to think it?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now how does it happen that I know not how we are abstracted from, and denied to, ourselves; and similarly am ignorant how we are restored and returned to ourselves?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: As if we are other persons, and elsewhere, when we seek, but fail to find, what we deposited in our memory; and are ourselves incapable of returning to ourselves, as if we were situated somewhere else; but afterwards return again, on finding ourselves out.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For where do we make our quest, except in our own selves?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: And what is it we search for, except our own selves?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: As if we were not actually at home in our persons, but had gone somewhither.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Do you not observe, even with alarm, so deep a mystery?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: And what is all this but our own nature—not what it has been, but such as it now is?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: And observe how much more we seek than we comprehend.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: I have often believed that I could understand a question which had been submitted to me, if I were to bestow thought upon it.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Well, I have bestowed the thought, but have not been able to solve the question; and many a time I have not so believed, and yet have been able to determine the point.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: The powers, then, of my own understanding have not been really known to me; nor, I apprehend, have they been to you either.
